race.name: L'Étape Slovenia by Tour de France
race.display_name: L'Étape Slovenia by Tour de France
race.tagline: Official Tour de France amateur cycling event in Slovenia's scenic Gorenjska region with pro-level closed-road routes.

climate.description: Early September in Kranj/Gorenjska typically features mild daytime temperatures with possible rain or fog in the foothills.

features: Hilly paved roads through Gorenjska region with climbs in the Julian Alps foothills

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 71. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 67.

course_description.character: Scenic, hilly loops through Gorenjska villages and foothills; traffic-free paved roads with pro support.
course_description.signature_challenge: Race's second loop via Besnica, Jamnik, Kropa, Golnik—hilly test in Julian Alps foothills.

logistics.lodging_strategy: Kranj hotels and Gorenjska options; event village on-site.
logistics.transport: Start/finish at Slovenski trg in Kranj; registrations via Prijavim.se with early-bird incentives.

history.origin_story: Founded 2022 as part of official L'Étape Series; annual September event hosted by Municipality of Kranj and Visit Kranj. Editions: 2022 (Sep 3-4), 2023, 2024 (Sep 7-8), 2025 (Sep 6-7), 2026 (Sep 5-6 planned).
notable_moments: 2025 early registration prizes: signed bibs, VIP starts.

biased_opinion.summary: A gem for road enthusiasts seeking Tour de France vibes in Slovenia's stunning foothills. The 150km Race delivers hilly suffering with pro polish, while Matej Mohorič's ambassadorship ties it to world-class heritage—perfect mid-tier adventure without Alpine brutality.

final_verdict.one_liner: Pro-level amateur road event with Slovenian flair and Julian Alps scenery.
final_verdict.should_you_race: L'Étape Slovenia stands out in the Étape series for its accessible yet challenging routes from Kranj, blending Tour branding, closed roads, and local hero appeal. Ideal for sportive riders chasing prestige without overcrowding.

citations.label: L'Étape Slovenia by Tour de France

youtube_data.search_query: L'Étape Slovenia by Tour de France gran fondo cycling Kranj

videos.title: Join Tadej Pogacar in Slovenia 🇸🇮 for l'Etape Slovenia by Tour de France
videos.channel: L'Etape Series by Tour de France
videos.description:
Welcome to Slovenia 🇸🇮, the home of champions !
On September 4th, experience the Tour de France Legend in Kranj and discover Tadej Pogacar training routes.

With the promo code "ifeelslovenia" get 20€ discount till 24th of July 2022.
More info about the event ➡️ https://bit.ly/LetapeSlovenia

---------

L'Etape by Tour de France is a series of amateur cycling events designed to experience the Tour de France Legend. It is the only event where you can race and feel like a Tour de France champion, and where you can enjoy the Tour de France unique atmosphere and passion.


“The Tour de France is the world's greatest cycling race. Each year it is passionately followed by fans around the world so it has naturally given rise to L'Etape by Tour de France series”, says Christian Prudhomme, Tour de France director. 

The series has now expanded to more than 20 international locations including:
🇫🇷 France
🇧🇷 Brasil
🇲🇽 México
🇨🇴 Colombia
🇨🇳 China
🇦🇺 Australia
🇹🇭 Thailand
🇨🇭 Switzerland
🇮🇩 Indonesia
🇪🇸 Spain
🇲🇾 Malaysia
🇸🇰 Slovakia
🇨🇿 Czech Republic
🇬🇧 UK
🇨🇦 Canada
🇺🇸 USA
🇳🇱 Netherlands
🇨🇾 Cyprus
🇸🇮 Slovenia
🇪🇨 Ecuador

Among these Tour de France official cycling events is of course L'Étape du Tour. With a peloton of 15 000 riders and 50 nationalities, it is Europe's biggest cycling event. Riding on the same routes and under the same conditions as Tour de France champions: this is the promise that L'Étape du Tour has kept since 1993.

Check out https://www.letapebytourdefrance.com/ for more information.

Join us on:
-Facebook 👉 https://www.facebook.com/letapebytourdefrance
-Instagram 👉 https://www.instagram.com/letapebytourdefrance/
-Strava 👉 https://www.strava.com/clubs/letapebyletourdefrance

videos.title: Primoz Roglic ZERO cycling to PRO in 1 year
videos.description:
Primož was born and raised in Trbovlje. 

He is the only child to father, Polde Roglic a former miner, and mother Anita Roglič.

At the age of 10, he was already skiing the local jump at Zagorje ob Savi.

“It was like flying. I liked the feeling. It’s something everyone dreams of doing,” Roglich said. “I wanted to be the best ski jumper in the world.”

At 17-year-old he won a gold medal at the world championships in 2007. A few weeks later, he ventured to Planica, site of one of the most notorious ski jumps in the world. It is here that Roglič suffered a huge crash with his shoulder and head taking the brunt of the impact resulting In being airlifted to hospital. 

Even though he broke his nose and suffered a concussion, he persisted with ski jumping for another four years. His team won the silver medal at the junior world cup in ski jumping in Kranj in 2008.  

He finally said goodbye to ski jumping in 2012. “I felt it was time for a change,” Roglič recounted. “I liked cycling and thought, why not? I will try to become professional.”

“Ski jumping is a different sport, but there is a lot of stretching, yoga, speed work and core training,” Roglič said. “The skills are different, but I think that those skills helped me as a cyclist.”

“When I was a ski jumper, I was not allowed to ride the bike because it would bulk you up,” Roglič said. “We did a lot of work with core strength, balance, flexibility and acrobatics. All that helps me on the bike.”

Hauptman, now a sport director at UAE-Emirates as well as the national coach for the Slovenian Olympic team, remembers it well.

He called me one day—I had no idea who he was—and he said, ‘hello, I am Primož and I want to become a professional bike racer,’” Hauptman said with a laugh. “I said, how old are you, 21? It’s not so easy, cycling, especially starting from zero. He told me he used to be a sportsman, and I thought, OK, if he was a runner or a cross-country skier, but when he said ski jumper, I just laughed. I told him, OK, get a bike and call me back. I never thought I would hear from him again.”

He sold his motorcycle and worked at a supermarket to save up money to buy a second-hand racing bike, and called Hauptman back. As Hauptman recalls, Roglič crashed more than he won.
“His big problem was staying in the group without crashing,” he said. “Once he was alone or on the climbs, you could see he had something special. He had a huge engine.”

The first hints of Roglič’s raw power came during training rides. One day, the team rode five hours at race speed and ended with a climb, and the only rider who could beat him was Jan Polanc.

“He made mistakes, but only once. He was mature, focused and very strong mentally. He believed in himself,” Hauptman said. “He really started at zero, and now he is a hero. It’s incredible.”

By 2013, just over a year after starting cycling, he had his first pro contract. He joined Adria Mobil, a Slovenian continental team. His first pro win came in his second season, taking a stage at the Tour d’Azerbaïdjan.

At 22, Roglich went in for testing at a top Slovenian sports lab. According to the German newspaper Deutsche Welle, Roglich revealed a V02max of 80.2, close to the top numbers posted by Chris Froome or Egan Bernal at the time.

In 2015 Jumbo-Visma sport director Frans Maasen recalls how the team was made aware of Primoz. “I got a call from a Slovenian friend, he said, ‘I have quite a story for you. I have found this ski jumper who has a huge motor and can ride like no one else.’ He wouldn’t stop talking about him,” “At first, I didn’t think much of it. We flew him to Netherlands to take a test. He was like a Ferrari.”

Roglič’s core strength and flexibility help him with his time trial position, so much so that he almost beat Dumoulin in his first grand tour time-trial stage.

joined Jumbo-Visma in 2016.

By 2016, just four years after he started to train and race, Roglič was at the WorldTour. Impressed by his capacity, the team brought him to the 2016 Jiro, his first grand tour. On his first day of his first grand tour, he nearly beat Doomoulin. Nine days later, he won a rolling, 40.5km individual time trial.

I have never seen a rider learn so fast what it takes to race at this level,” said Jumbo-Visma sports director Addy Engels. “You have to remember that he did not start racing until he was 21. And now he has won a grand tour in only his fifth one. That is impressive.”

“He’s pretty hard to read,” Bennett said. “He keeps his cards pretty close to his chest. But what he can do on the bike is impressive. He is almost freakish in how strong he is.”

Teammates describe Roglič, by use words like “focused,” “driven,” and someone who is “detailed-oriented.”

“He’s a great leader to work for. He stays calm in every situation, and that carries over to us,” said teammate Sepp Kuss. “Things don’t faze him. He brushes things off. He says things that are uniquely Primož.”

quotes.text: The cycling route will take you through my hometown Kranj and you will get to cycle along the beautiful roads which help to shape the way I cycle.
quotes.source_channel: L'Etape Series by Tour de France

additional_quotes.text: The cycling route will take you through my hometown clan espiricomandi you will get to cycle along the beautiful roads which help to shape the way i cycle
additional_quotes.source_channel: L'Etape Series by Tour de France

rider_intel.search_text: L'Étape Slovenia by Tour de France is a 75-mile gravel event with 5900 feet of elevation in Kranj, Slovenia. The race route passes through roads that helped shape professional cyclist Tadej Pogačar's development, as he mentions riders will cycle through his hometown of Kranj and the surrounding area. The event is positioned as offering beautiful roads through the Slovenian countryside that were formative for one of cycling's biggest stars. Limited specific course details are available from rider accounts, but the event promises scenic terrain through the region that produced world-class cycling talent.
